sections.heading: The Three Comparison Symbols
sections.content: We use three symbols to show how two numbers compare. Greater than (>) means the first number is bigger. Less than (<) means the first number is smaller. Equal (=) means both numbers are the same.

steps: The open side faces the bigger number
The pointed end faces the smaller number

sections.heading: The Crocodile Trick
sections.content: Imagine the > and < symbols are a hungry crocodile's mouth. The crocodile always wants to eat the bigger number! So the open side (the wide part) always faces the bigger number.

steps: When numbers are equal, use the = sign
The crocodile can't decide — they're equal!

sections.heading: Reading Comparison Sentences
sections.content: Read comparison sentences from left to right, just like reading words. '5 > 3' is read as 'five is greater than three.' '2 < 9' is read as 'two is less than nine.' '7 = 7' is read as 'seven equals seven.'

common_pitfalls.correction: The open (wide) side always faces the bigger number — like a hungry mouth
Always read left to right: 4 < 8 means '4 is less than 8'
When both numbers are the same, use = not > or <
